audit_status.review_note: Compact complete draft reviewed as Zophar's second speech: urgency of reply, short joy of the wicked, swallowed wealth, poison imagery, exposure by heaven/earth, and allotted fate. Marked as Zophar's speech, not final theology.

verses.restored: Zophar the Naamathite answered and said:
Therefore my troubled thoughts answer me, because of my haste within me.
I hear a correction that insults me, and a spirit from my understanding answers me.
Do you not know this from old, since humanity was placed on earth,
that the shouting of the wicked is short, and the joy of the godless is only for a moment?
Though his height rises to the heavens and his head reaches the clouds,
he perishes forever like his own dung; those who saw him say, Where is he?
He flies away like a dream and is not found; he is chased away like a vision of the night.
The eye that saw him will not see him again, and his place will behold him no more.
His children will seek the favor of the poor, and his hands will return his wealth.
His bones are full of his youth, but it will lie down with him in the dust.
Though evil is sweet in his mouth, though he hides it under his tongue,
though he spares it and does not let it go, holding it in the midst of his palate,
his food is turned in his bowels; it becomes venom within him.
He swallows wealth, but vomits it up; the Divine drives it out of his belly.
He sucks the venom of cobras; the tongue of a viper kills him.
He will not look on the streams, the rivers flowing with honey and curds.
He returns what he labored for and does not swallow it; according to the wealth of his exchange, he will not rejoice.
For he crushed and abandoned the poor; he seized a house he did not build.
Because he knew no quiet in his belly, he will not escape with what he desires.
Nothing escaped his eating; therefore his prosperity will not endure.
In the fullness of his abundance he will be distressed; every hand of trouble will come against him.
To fill his belly, the Divine will send fierce anger against him and rain it upon him as his food.
He flees from an iron weapon; a bronze bow pierces him.
He draws it out, and it comes from his body; the glittering point comes from his gall. Terrors come upon him.
All darkness is stored up for his treasures; a fire not blown will consume him; what remains in his tent will suffer harm.
The heavens will reveal his guilt, and the earth will rise against him.
The increase of his house will depart, flowing away in the day of his anger.
This is the portion of a wicked person from the Divine, the inheritance appointed to him by God.
